topic: How cnogo execution works with the integrated context graph

sources.description: ContextGraph class: index(), query(), impact(), review_impact(), search(), and 7 other public methods
12 phase modules: structure, symbols, imports, calls, heritage, types, exports, flows, impact, dead_code, coupling, community
GraphStorage with SQLite backend, FTS5 virtual table, file hash tracking
11 graph-* CLI subcommands exposing all graph queries
post_commit_graph() hook triggers incremental reindex after git commit
Hook registration for PostToolUse Bash matcher

summary: The context graph is a separate SQLite database (.cnogo/graph.db) that models code structure — files, functions, classes, methods, imports, calls, heritage, types, exports — as a directed graph with 10 node types and 11 relationship types.
Integration occurs at 3 points: (1) automatic impact analysis during /review, (2) PostCommit hook for incremental reindexing, (3) 11 CLI subcommands for ad-hoc queries.

The indexing pipeline has 10 sequential phases: structure → symbols → imports → calls → heritage → types → exports → flows → FTS rebuild → hash update. Incremental reindexing compares SHA256 file hashes to skip unchanged files.
The graph and memory engines are deliberately decoupled — separate databases, no bidirectional data flow. The memory engine tracks issues/tasks; the graph tracks code dependencies.
During /review, _graph_impact_section() auto-indexes the graph then runs BFS-based impact analysis on changed files, populating REVIEW.json with affected files, symbols, and per-file breakdowns.
Graph queries (dead code, coupling, communities, flows, search) are available as standalone CLI commands that don't require memory engine initialization.

recommendation.summary: The context graph is well-integrated for passive analysis (/review blast radius, PostCommit freshness) but underutilized for active workflow assistance (/plan, /implement, /discuss). The next high-value integration would be using graph queries during /plan to auto-suggest affected files and during /implement to validate scope discipline.

options.name: Deepen /plan integration
options.description: Use graph-impact and graph-context during /plan to auto-suggest file scope for tasks, identify coupling risks, and detect dead code candidates for cleanup.
options.risk: Increases plan generation time; graph must be indexed before planning

options.name: Add /implement scope validation
options.description: After each task, use graph-impact to verify that changes don't affect files outside the declared task scope. Flag unintended blast radius.
options.risk: False positives from fuzzy call resolution (0.5 confidence edges)

options.name: Enrich /discuss with graph context
options.description: During /discuss, auto-query the graph for related code (callers, callees, heritage) to surface architectural constraints before decisions are made.
options.risk: May add noise for greenfield features with no existing code

options.name: Graph-powered test suggestion
options.description: Use impact analysis to suggest which test files should be run based on changed files, rather than running the full suite.
options.risk: Requires reliable CALLS edges to test functions; current confidence model may miss indirect test dependencies

openQuestions: Should graph queries during /plan be opt-in or automatic?
How should fuzzy (0.5 confidence) CALLS edges be treated in scope validation — include or exclude?
Should the graph support cross-language analysis (e.g., Python calling shell scripts) in a future milestone?
